front: What is the formal definition of a closed set in a metric space (X, d)?
back: A subset F of a metric space (X, d) is closed if its complement, X \ F, is an open set.

front: How is a closed set related to its own limit points?
back: A set F is closed if and only if it contains all of its limit points. That is, F' ⊆ F, where F' is the derived set (set of all limit points) of F.

front: What is the closure of a set A in a metric space, and how is it defined?
back: The closure of a set A, denoted Ā or Cl(A), is the smallest closed set containing A. It is equal to the union of A and its set of limit points, A ∪ A'.

front: Provide an equivalent characterization of a closed set using sequences.
back: A set F is closed if and only if for every convergent sequence (xₙ) in F (i.e., each xₙ ∈ F), the limit of the sequence also belongs to F.

front: True or False: In any metric space, the whole space X and the empty set ∅ are both closed.
back: True. Their complements are ∅ and X, respectively. Since both ∅ and X are open sets (by definition), it follows that X and ∅ are also closed. They are therefore called clopen sets.
